title: Somānanda II: Parātriśikātātparyadīpikā and Śāktavijñāna
author: Somānanda (II)
date_estimate: uncertain; likely 2nd millennium CE (a later Somānanda, distinct from the c. 900 CE founder of the Pratyabhijñā)
summary: Two works grouped under the name Somānanda: the Parātriśikātātparyadīpikā, a commentary elucidating the 'inner purport' of the short but highly esteemed Parātriśikā (Parātrīśikā), the celebrated dialogue between Bhairava and the Goddess on the supreme Power (Parā) drawn from the Rudrayāmala; and the Śāktavijñāna, a non-dual Śākta-Trika exposition of the knowledge (vijñāna) of Śakti. Together they treat the metaphysics of the highest goddess-energy, the emanation of the phonemic cosmos, and the recognition of consciousness as supreme reality.
history: The famous Somānanda is the early (c. 900 CE) author of the Śivadṛṣṭi and teacher of Utpaladeva; the 'Somānanda II' label here flags a later author of the same name to whom these Parātriśikā-related works are attributed, since the great Somānanda's authentic oeuvre is the Śivadṛṣṭi. The Parātriśikā itself attracted commentaries by Abhinavagupta (Vivaraṇa and Laghuvṛtti) and later Kashmirian and Śākta authors; attributions of further glosses such as this Dīpikā are sometimes uncertain.

title: Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma: Parātriśikāvivṛti
author: Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma (Lakṣmīrāma)
date_estimate: uncertain; likely 17th-19th c. CE (a late Kashmirian Śaiva scholar)
summary: A vivṛti (detailed gloss) on the Parātriśikā (Parātrīśikā), the short Trika scripture in which Bhairava expounds to the Goddess the nature of the supreme Power (Parā), the anuttara state, and the deployment of phonemes (mātṛkā) underlying creation. The commentary unpacks the dense esoteric verses in the non-dual Kashmirian framework.
history: The Parātriśikā, regarded as the essence of Trika, was commented upon most famously by Abhinavagupta; Rājānaka Lakṣmīrāma belongs to the later Kashmirian pandit lineage ('Rājānaka' being an honorific borne by Kashmirian Brahman scholars) who continued to transmit and gloss the Trika and Pratyabhijñā corpus into the early modern period.

title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 1
author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
date_estimate: late 18th c. CE (c. 1775)
summary: An encyclopaedic 'Ocean of Preliminary Ritual Practice' (puraścaryā = the preparatory observances qualifying a practitioner to use a mantra), compiling rules for initiation, mantra-japa, homa, and the disciplines preceding tantric worship. Volume 1 covers the opening sections on the qualifications, times, places, and procedures of puraścaryā. The work draws extensively on Tantras, Purāṇas, and dharmaśāstra.
history: Composed by Pratāpasiṃha Śāha (Pratap Singh Shah), the 18th-century king of Nepal and a devout Tantric, who is named as its royal author; it became a standard reference manual for tantric ritual practice and was printed in multiple volumes. Manuscripts survive in Nepal and have been digitised in preservation programmes.

title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 2
author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
date_estimate: late 18th c. CE (c. 1775)
summary: The second volume of the 'Ocean of Preliminary Ritual Practice', continuing the systematic treatment of puraścaryā and tantric sadhana: rules of mantra repetition, oblation (homa), feeding, ritual offerings, and associated observances. It assembles citations from Tantras, Purāṇas, and smṛti to authorise the procedures.
history: Part of the multi-volume digest authored by King Pratāpasiṃha Śāha of Nepal in the late 18th century; widely used as an authoritative compendium of tantric ritual, transmitted in manuscript and later print editions.

title: Puraścaryārṇava, volume 3
author: Pratāpasiṃha Śāha Deva (Pratap Singh Shah, King of Nepal)
date_estimate: late 18th c. CE (c. 1775)
summary: The third volume of the 'Ocean of Preliminary Ritual Practice', concluding the digest's exposition of puraścaryā and related tantric worship, including the consummation of mantra-sadhana and the rituals that complete the preparatory cycle. Like the other volumes it is a vast anthology of authoritative scriptural citations organised topically.
history: Final portion of the encyclopaedic ritual digest composed by King Pratāpasiṃha Śāha of Nepal in the late 18th century, a landmark reference work for Hindu tantric ritual practice.

title: Kṣemarāja: Parāprāveśikā
author: Kṣemarāja
date_estimate: early 11th c. CE
summary: A concise prose primer of non-dual Kashmirian Śaiva (Trika) philosophy by Kṣemarāja, opening with an introductory aphorism followed by a short gloss. 'Entry into the Supreme' centres on Śakti in her aspect as Parā (the Supreme Power) and lays out the tattvas (categories of universal manifestation) within the Śaivādvaita framework of consciousness as the sole reality.
history: Kṣemarāja was the foremost disciple of Abhinavagupta and one of the most prolific systematisers of Kashmir Shaivism, also author of the Pratyabhijñāhṛdayam, Spandanirṇaya, and Śivasūtravimarśinī. The Parāprāveśikā functions as an accessible doctrinal introduction to the school's metaphysics.

title: Mahānirvāṇa Tantra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. late 18th c. CE (disputed; some place portions earlier, but the received text is generally dated to the 1700s, likely Bengal)
summary: A celebrated Śākta Tantra cast as a dialogue between Śiva and the Goddess (Pārvatī), expounding the worship of the supreme Brahman as the Goddess in the Kali age, mantra and ritual practice, the pañcatattva, social and moral duties, and a notably reformist, dharma-conscious ethical code. It is unusual among Tantras for its strong emphasis on conventional morality and law.
history: The text rose to prominence largely through the edition with Hariharānanda Bhāratī's commentary (Hariharānanda was the guru of Rammohan Roy) and the English translation by Sir John Woodroffe ('Arthur Avalon'); first printed in the 19th century. Its date and integrity are debated, with many scholars regarding it as a relatively late composition, possibly partly shaped by 18th-19th century reformist concerns.

title: Nīlakaṇṭha: Kriyāsāra, volume 2
author: Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya
genre: philosophical treatise / commentary on the Brahmasūtra
date_estimate: uncertain; generally placed in the medieval-to-early modern period (post-Śaṅkara), perhaps c. 14th-16th c. CE
summary: A major Vīraśaiva work that expounds the 'Essence of Practice', presenting Vīraśaiva ritual and doctrine, and read alongside Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya's interpretation of Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra from a Śivādvaita (qualified non-dualist Śaiva) standpoint. Volume 2 continues the systematic exposition of doctrine and observance.
history: Authored by Nīlakaṇṭha Śivācārya, a Vīraśaiva theologian; the Sanskrit text was critically edited by R. Ramasastri and N. S. Venkatanathacharya and published by the University of Mysore (1954-1958). It is an important source for Vīraśaiva (Lingāyat) Vedānta.

title: Sāhib Kaul: Kalpavṛkṣa
author: Sāhib Kaul (Sāhib Kaula)
tradition: Hindu, Kashmir Shaivism / Śākta (Pratyabhijñā fused with Advaita Vedānta)
date_estimate: mid-17th c. CE (author fl. 1642-1667+)
summary: An extraordinary 'Wish-fulfilling Tree', a multilingual crossword-like figure poem (carmen cancellatum) originally displayed on a large cloth, in which hidden inscribed texts ('intexts') are read out of a grid using roughly thirty languages. It is described as the largest carmen cancellatum known in world literature, a virtuoso devotional-poetic creation.
history: Composed in 17th-century Kashmir by Sāhib Kaul, a prolific and ambitious Śākta-Śaiva poet who also wrote the Devīnāmavilāsa, the Śyāmāpaddhati, and several ritual handbooks. Alexis Sanderson has argued Sāhib Kaul and his lineage introduced eastern-Indian Śākta elements into Kashmir and fused Advaita Vedānta with Pratyabhijñā. The Sanskrit text was edited for the first time by Jürgen Hanneder.

title: Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇavatantra with the commentary called Setubandha
author: Commentary (Setubandha) by Bhāskararāya Makhin; mūla tantra anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: mūla tantra: 1st millennium / early-mid 2nd millennium CE (a portion of the Vāmakeśvaratantra); Setubandha commentary: 18th c. CE (Bhāskararāya, c. 1690-1785)
summary: The Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava ('Ocean of the Sixteen Nityā goddesses'), a principal Śrīvidyā scripture of about four hundred verses in five chapters dealing with the external and internal worship of the goddess Tripurasundarī, forming the first half of the Vāmakeśvaratantra. The accompanying Setubandha ('Building of the Bridge') is Bhāskararāya's authoritative technical commentary on mantraśāstra and the goddess's worship.
history: The Setubandha is Bhāskararāya Makhin's magnum opus; he was an 18th-century polymath who composed around forty works across Vedānta, Mīmāṃsā, grammar, Nyāya, and mantraśāstra, and remains the most prestigious commentator in the living Śrīvidyā tradition. Śivānanda earlier wrote the first South Indian commentary on the same tantra.

title: Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara: Īśvarapratyabhijñākaumudī
author: Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara
date_estimate: uncertain; a later commentary on Utpaladeva's IPK, likely 2nd millennium CE
summary: A commentary (the 'Moonlight on the Recognition of the Lord') on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational philosophical treatise of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism. It elucidates the doctrine that liberation comes through recognising one's own self as identical with Śiva, the supreme conscious agent, engaging the dialectic with Buddhist epistemology.
history: The Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā (c. early 10th c. CE) is the cornerstone of the Pratyabhijñā and was most famously glossed by Utpaladeva himself (Vṛtti, Vivṛti) and by Abhinavagupta (Vimarśinī, Vivṛtivimarśinī). This Kaumudī by Bhaṭṭāraka Sundara represents a further, less widely studied commentary in that tradition.

title: Devanandanasiṃha: Śāktapramoda
author: Deva Nandan Singh (Devanandana Siṃha)
date_estimate: 19th c. CE
summary: A liturgical compendium ('Delight of the Śāktas') gathering sixteen independent ritual manuals. The first ten are devoted to the Ten Mahāvidyās (Kālī, Tārā, Ṣoḍaśī, Bhuvaneśvarī, Chinnamastā, Mātaṅgī, Tripurabhairavī, Dhūmāvatī, Bagalāmukhī, Kamalā); further sections cover the Kumārī Tantra and a pentad of deities (Gaṇeśa, Viṣṇu, Śiva, Durgā, Sūrya). Each provides dhyāna, mantra and yantra, ritual sequences, kavacas, and litanies of epithets.
history: Compiled and first published by Raja Deva Nandan Singh, an aristocratic zamindar of Muzaffarpur, Bihar, in the 19th century; it remains a widely used practical handbook for Śākta worship. A modern critical edition with English introduction was prepared by Madhu Khanna.

title: Narasiṃhaṭhakkura: Tārābhaktisudhārṇava
author: Narasiṃha Ṭhakkura (Narasimha, disciple of Gadādhara)
date_estimate: c. 1668 CE (later 17th c.)
summary: An 'Ocean of the Nectar of Devotion to Tārā', a digest of tantric rituals for the worship of the goddess Tārā, one of the ten Mahāvidyās. Written mainly in lucid verse with occasional prose, it is divided into eleven tarangas ('waves') and compiles the dhyāna, mantras, yantras, and ritual procedures of Tārā's cult, treating theory only incidentally.
history: Composed by Narasiṃha Ṭhakkura, a disciple of Gadādhara, of the line of Ravikara of Mithila and fifth in descent from Govinda Ṭhakkura (author of the Kāvyapradīpa); he flourished c. 1668 CE. It draws on the Tārātantra, Brahmayāmala, Rudrayāmala, and related scriptures and is a standard source for Tārā worship.

title: Lalitā Sahasranāma
author: anonymous/traditional (framed as revealed by the Vāgdevatās; dialogue of Hayagrīva and Agastya)
genre: stotra (sahasranāma, litany of a thousand names)
date_estimate: c. 1st millennium CE (often placed mid-to-late 1st millennium; embedded in the Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa, date disputed)
summary: A litany of one thousand names of the goddess Lalitā (Mahātripurasundarī), the supreme deity of the Śrīvidyā tradition, set within the Lalitopākhyāna of the Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa as a dialogue between Hayagrīva and the sage Agastya. The names encode the goddess's attributes, her form, the Śrīcakra, kuṇḍalinī, and esoteric Śrīvidyā doctrine, and the hymn is among the most widely recited Śākta stotras.
history: Transmitted as part of the Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa's Lalitopākhyāna; its precise date is hard to fix and is generally regarded as later than the Purāṇa's core. It is the basis of Bhāskararāya's famous commentary Saubhāgyabhāskara and is central to living Śrīvidyā devotion.

title: Jñānaprakāśa (Śālivāṭipuranivāsi): Śivāgamādimāhātmya
author: Jñānaprakāśa (of Śālivāṭipura)
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval-to-early modern South Indian Śaiva author (likely 2nd millennium CE)
summary: A compilation ('Greatness of the Śivāgamas and related scriptures', also styled Śivāgamādimāhātmyasaṃgraha) of passages drawn from the Purāṇas and the Śaivāgamas assembled in order to demonstrate and establish the authority and validity of the Śaivāgamas as revealed scripture. It is an apologetic-doctrinal work defending the canonical standing of the Āgamic corpus.
history: Authored by Jñānaprakāśa, associated with Śālivāṭipura in the South Indian Śaiva Siddhānta milieu; the work serves the polemical purpose of vindicating Āgamic scriptural authority within the dualist Śaiva Siddhānta tradition.

title: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha: Vyomavyāpistava
author: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II) (commentator); the Vyomavyāpin/Vyomavyāpistava hymn itself is a Śaiva mantra-stotra
date_estimate: commentary c. late 10th c. CE (Rāmakaṇṭha II fl. c. 950-1000 CE)
summary: The Vyomavyāpistava ('Hymn to Him who pervades the void/space') is a Śaiva hymn-cum-mantra (the Vyomavyāpin mantra) addressed to Śiva as the all-pervading reality, here transmitted with the learned commentary of Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha. It expounds the theology of Śiva's pervasion of the cosmos within the Śaiva Siddhānta framework.
history: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (II) was a leading 10th-century Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta theologian, author of commentaries such as the Nareśvaraparīkṣāprakāśa and the Kiraṇavṛtti and glosses on Sadyojyotis's works. His commentary on the Vyomavyāpin/Vyomavyāpistava situates the hymn in the dualist Śaiva exegetical tradition.

title: Rāmatoṣaṇa (compiler): Prāṇatoṣiṇī, Bhaktikāṇḍa
author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṃkāra)
summary: The Prāṇatoṣiṇī ('That which satisfies the life-breath / Prāṇa') is a large tantric digest compiling extracts from numerous Tantras on worship, sādhana, and ritual. This portion, the Bhaktikāṇḍa ('section on devotion'), gathers material on devotional worship and the cultivation of bhakti within the tantric system.
history: Compiled in 1820 by Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṃkāra) in Bengal, it became one of the most-cited 19th-century Śākta tantric reference works, valued for preserving quotations from many otherwise scattered or lost Tantras.

title: Rāmatoṣaṇa (compiler): Prāṇatoṣiṇī, part 6
author: Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya (Vidyālaṃkāra)
summary: Part 6 of the Prāṇatoṣiṇī, the large Bengali Śākta tantric digest, continuing its anthology of citations from many Tantras on ritual worship, mantra, initiation, and sādhana arranged topically. The digest as a whole is organised into sections (kāṇḍas/parts) covering the full range of tantric practice.
history: Belongs to the same 1820 compilation by Rāmatoṣaṇa Bhaṭṭācārya; the work is prized by scholars as a repository of quotations from numerous Tantras, many otherwise rare.

title: Paratantra (Parātantra)
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: unknown
summary: A text transmitted under the title 'Paratantra'. The bare title is ambiguous: it could denote a Tantra so named (a Śaiva/Śākta scripture in the Tantric corpus) or, less likely, a doctrinal treatise. Without a distinguishing author, edition, or incipit the precise identity and contents cannot be confidently established. (Note: 'paratantra' is also a Yogācāra philosophical term for the 'dependent nature', but here it appears as a work title.)
history: Could not be reliably identified from available sources. Several distinct works and a philosophical concept share the name, so attribution, date, and transmission remain undetermined.

title: Sakalāgamasārasaṅgraha
author: anonymous/traditional (compiler unidentified in available sources)
date_estimate: uncertain; likely medieval-to-early modern (2nd millennium CE)
summary: A 'Compendium of the Essence of All Āgamas', a digest distilling the substance of the Āgamic (Śaiva/Tantric) scriptures, presenting in summary form the doctrines and ritual prescriptions drawn from the broader Āgama corpus. It belongs to the genre of synthetic handbooks that organise Āgamic teaching for practitioners and students.
history: The specific compiler, date, and recension were not confidently identifiable from available sources; the title indicates a South Indian / Tantric Āgamic milieu where such summarising saṅgraha works were produced to make the vast Āgama literature accessible.
